results:
The drug entrapment efficiency, drug loading, particle size and zeta potential were investigated. The surface morphology was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Mean particle size of nanoparticles was altered by changing the drug:polymer ratio and stirring speed. Addition of surfactants showed a promise to increase drug loading, encapsulation efficiency, and decreased particle size. The drug release pattern consisted of two phases releasing about 40% (within first 24 h) followed by a slow releasing phase (up to 90%) within next 48 h. The release data was fitted in various kinetic models (zero-order, first-order, and Higuchi's kinetics) indicated a controlled drug release. Accelerated stability studies (ICH guidelines) revealed that the GPZ-loaded nanoparticles were stable at the end of 6 months.

3.1. Drug polymer interaction study
For studying the probable interaction between drug, polymer and excepients the FT-IR (Shimadzu 8400-S) spectroscopy was used. The spectra of pure GPZ was taken, which showed peaks of -NH stretching (3274 cm-1), CH stretching (3113 cm-1), OC (1705 cm-1), CC aromatic (1596 cm-1) C-H deformation (1467-1430 cm-1) SO2-NH (1352 cm-1). Similar peaks were seen in GPZ-loaded PLGA NPs. Therefore, the study indicates no interaction between drug, polymer, and excipients in drug-loaded nanoparticles.
3.2. Formulation optimization and preparation of nanoparticles
The formulation was optimized for further studies, on the basis of good morphology (in terms of particle size and surface properties) high entrapment efficiency and high drug contents.
Structurally GPZ has a complex chemical structure, having polycyclic rings imparting lipophilic characters. Thus GPZ shows solubility in various organic solvents such as methanol and dichloromethane and very less solubility in water. Initially two organic solvent methanol and dichloromethane were tried for preparation of GPZ loaded PLGA nanoparticles. When methanol was used in preparation of nanoparticles, a very low drug entrapment (23.25 +- 1.36%) and drug content (8.36 +- 2.15%) were obtained with drug polymer ratio 1:2; this was mainly due to more hydrophilic nature of the methanol. On the other hand, with dichloromethane sufficient GPZ entrapment and GPZ content were observed. This was probably due to its hydrophobic nature which enabled GPZ to retain in the hydrophobic nanoparticle matrix. Hence dichloromethane was used as an organic phase to prepare NPs using a modified emulsification solvent evaporation technique.
Different GPZ: PLGA ratios varied from 1:4, 1:2, 1:1, and 2:1 (formulations F1, F2, F3, and F4) were used in the study. The results (Table 1) showed that formulation number F1-F4, an increase in initial amount of PLGA increases in GPZ entrapment, GPZ loading and mean particle size. The formulation F2 renders 57.23 +- 3.16% GPZ entrapment and 24.45 +- 3.13% GPZ content with 522 +- 35.24 nm particle size showing comparatively lower particle size as compared to F3 and F4.

The effect of stirring speed (300-3000 rpm) on entrapment efficiency, drug content and particle size was studied. An increase in GPZ entrapment efficiency and GPZ contents with lowered mean particle sizes (Table 2) were observed when the stirring speed was increased . GPZ loaded PLGA nanoparticles prepared with stirring speed of 3000 rpm demonstrated best entrapment efficiency and drug content together with lowest particle size.

Two non-ionic surfactants polyvinyl alcohol (formulation A) and polysorbate-80 (formulation B) and in fixed concentration (0.5%, w/v) were incorporated to further optimize the formulation . Similarly formulation without any surfactant (formulation C) was also prepared for comparing the effect of surfactant on entrapment efficiency, drug content and particle size. Formulations A and B showed a remarkable increase in entrapment efficiency and drug content and a marginal decrease in mean particle size as compare to formulation C (Table 3). Observations reveal that polyvinyl alcohol (0.5%, w/v) is a better surfactant as compared to polysorbate-80 (0.5%, w/v) in terms of entrapment efficiency, drug content and particle size. Enhanced entrapment efficiency and drug contents in formulation A are probably because PVA have a greater tendency to migrate to the surface of PLGA nanoparticles than polysorbate-80. PVA stabilizes the nanoparticles surface more effectively and hence a lower particle size is attained. Thus, for further study, polyvinyl alcohol (0.5%, w/v) selected as stabilizer.

3.3. Particle size
The mean diameter of all the nanoparticles formulations were determined and are listed in Table 1, Table 2, Table 3. The nanoparticles prepared by using dichloromethane as organic solvent, drug polymer ratio 1:2, stirring speed 3000 rpm and PVA as stabilizer, have 522 +- 5.24 nm sizes with optimum entrapment efficiency.
3.4. Zeta potential
The zeta potential is an index of stability of nanoparticles. The higher the magnitude, irrespective of the charge type, higher the stability is expected. All the formulations of nanoparticles exhibited low zeta potentials ranging from -6.57 +- 2.1 mV to -31.03 +- 1.7 mV (Table 3). Generally highly negative zeta potential values are expected for pure PLGA nanoparticles due to presence of carboxyl groups on the polymeric chain extremities. However, in formulation A and B the zeta potential value is close to zero. The factor which might be responsible for such an effect can be the presence of residual stabilizers on the nanoparticles surface. The residual stabilizer on the nanoparticles surface can create a shield between the surface and surrounding medium. Moreover the stabilizers would mask the possible charged groups existing on the particle surface leading to zeta potential values close to zero .
3.5. Surface morphology
Fig. 1 represents the SEM images of the particles as a function of polymer concentration. The particles were nearly spherical in shape. The image clearly shows the numerous pores on the surface of nanoparticles. This porous nature of nanoparticles surface is the major characteristics of PLGA nanoparticles responsible for the controlled release of entrapped material from the nanoparticles.
3.6. Release profile of GPZ
A sustained release formulation should release the required quantity of drug with predetermined kinetics in order to maintain an effective drug plasma concentration. To achieve this, the delivery system should be formulated so that it releases the drug in a predetermined and reproducible manner. In vitro drug release study formulations A and B were carried out in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). Fig. 2 shows the in vitro release profile of GPZ formulations. GPZ release from nanoparticles (prepared by using PVA and polysorbate-80 stabilizer) appeared to have two components. First an initial exponential phase releasing 4.35 mg (8.7%) and 4.89 mg (9.78%) of the drug in 1st hour which is almost identical with the conventional dose of a 5 mg tablet, followed by a slow phase release up to 19% of the drug in 12 h, respectively, from formulations A and B. The initial release phase of GPZ was probably due to GPZ which was adsorbed or close to the surface of the nanoparticles and due to diffusion of the dissolved GPZ within the PLGA core of the nanoparticles in to the release medium. After 12 h, the release rate increased slightly until the 24th hour probably because of a lag phase when sufficient polymer erosion has taken place. Finally the release slowed but continued until the 72 h (91% drug release), because the diffusion controlled release pattern was completed and the erosion controlled release was probably started. On the basis of these observations we could articulated that a sustained-release pattern was observed throughout the 72 h release study.
The in vitro release behavior of formulation A (r2 = 0.9826) and formulation B (r2 = 0.9603) was also compared with the theoretical (predictive) profile and found to be identical; a very close relationship was noted between the test and theoretical release patterns (Fig. 2). The zero-order rate describes the systems where the drug release rate is independent of its concentration. Fig. 2 shows the cumulative amount of drug release vs. time for zero-order kinetics indicating that the concentration was nearly independent of drug release probably in first 24 h. The release constants were calculated from the slope of the appropriate plots, and the regression coefficients (r2) were determined (Table 4).

The first-order describes the release from systems where the release rate is concentration-dependent, is illustrated by Fig. 3, which shows the log of cumulative percent drug retained vs. time. The release data is also following the first-order kinetics; this explains why the drug diffuses at a comparatively slower rate as the distance for diffusion increases between 24 and 72 h. However, drug release was found to be very close to zero-order kinetics, Fig. 2 verifies the correlation of the GPZ release profile with the theoretical profile.
Higuchi's model describes the release of drugs from an insoluble matrix as a square root of a time-dependent process based on Fickian diffusion. Drug diffuses at a comparatively slower rate as the distance for diffusion increases, which is referred to as square root kinetics (or Higuchi's kinetics; Fig. 4).
The cumulative % release after 72 h was found to be 91.3 and 94.23% from formulation A and B, respectively. In addition the surface morphology of formulation A is superior as compare to B. thus it is revealed that PVA is better stabilizer as compared to polysorbate-80 for the preparation of GPZ loaded PLGA nanoparticles.
3.7. Stability study
GPZ loaded PLGA NPs containing 0.5% PVA (formulation A) as surfactant was subjected to stability study. Stability studies according to ICH guidelines reveal that NPs were stable at the end of 6 months in all the test conditions (Table 5).

No significant changes in particle size, GPZ entrapment, GPZ content and release profile were observed after the end of 1, 3 and 6 months. The release kinetics was also found identical in stability studies.
conclusion:
The Controlled release biodegradable nanoparticles can be prepared by selecting the proper processing variables.
Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by a high blood glucose concentration (hyperglycemia) caused by insulin deficiency and it is often combined with insulin resistance . Non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (NIDDM) represents a heterogeneous group comprising milder form of diabetes that occurs predominately in adults and vast majority of diabetic patients have NIDDM . Glipizide, 1-cyclohexyl-3-[[4-[2-[[(5-methylpyrazin-2-yl)carbonyl]amino]ethyl]-phenyl]sulphonyl]urea, is a potential second generation oral hypoglycemic agent widely used for the treatment of non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (NIDDM) by stimulating insulin release from pancreatic b cells , . Subsequent research work revealed that it has a good general tolerability, comparatively low incidence of hypoglycemia and low rate of secondary failure , . In addition it has potential for slowing the progression of diabetic retinopathy . For these reasons, GPZ appears to be a drug of choice in long term sulfonylurea therapy for the control of NIDDM.
In general, rapid gastrointestinal (GI) absorption is required for oral hypoglycemic drugs, to prevent a sudden increase in the blood glucose level after food intake in patients with diabetes mellitus . However, the GI absorption rate of GPZ, in conventional dosage form (i.e. tablets), appears to be rather slow . Several studies using healthy volunteers or patients revealed that the time to reach peak serum GPZ concentration ranged from 0.5 to 1 h following oral administration of the GPZ tablet . Slow absorption of drug usually originates from either poor permeability of the drug across the GI membrane. The dose of GPZ is 5 mg tid, and hence there is always a need for the development of the sustained release patient complaint formulation of GPZ, as it is short acting sulfonylurea. Nanoparticles are one of the multiparticulate delivery systems and are prepared to obtain prolonged or controlled drug delivery and to improve bioavailability as well as stability . Nanoparticles can also offer advantages like limiting fluctuations within therapeutic range, reducing side effects, decreasing dosing frequency, and improving patient compliance .
Poly(lactic-co-glycolic)acid (PLGA) is a biodegradable polymer and used for preparation of controlled release dosage forms. Release kinetics from PLGA and other biodegradable polymers are controlled by diffusion, erosion or a combination thereof, and are dependent on the polymer (Mw, copolymer ratio and crystallinity), drug properties, as well as the device characteristics (preparation conditions, particle size, morphology, porosity and drug loading) and the dissolution conditions , .
The aim of this study was to formulate and optimize PLGA nanoparticles containing GPZ to achieve a sustained release profile suitable for per oral administration with enhanced efficacy and which could overcome the drawbacks of GPZ delivery through conventional dosage forms.

The present research work proposed a novel nanoparticulate formulation of glipizide. The results indicated that GPZ can be entrapped within PLGA nanoparticles if PVA (0.5%) is used as stabilizer. The release profile of the nanoparticles consists of two distinctive phases, an initial exponential phase followed by a slow release phase. The present study is concluded that the prepared nanoparticles could be explored sustained release preparation for effective management of type II diabetes mellitus with reduced dose frequency, decreased side effects and improved patient compliance.
materials_methods:
PLGA nanoparticles (PLGA NPs) were prepared by a modified emulsification solvent evaporation technique. Subsequent study shows no interaction of GPZ with PLGA (FT-IR study). Various formulation parameters such as stirring speed (300-3000 rpm), drug:polymer ratio (1:4 to 2:1), with addition of surfactants (0.5%, w/v polyvinyl alcohol/polysorbate-80) were studies for particle size, drug loading, and encapsulation efficiency.

Poly(lactic-co-glycolic)acid (PLGA 50:50) (Mw = 18,000 D) and glipizide were procured as gift sample from SPARK Laboratory, India. Polysorbate-80, dichloromethane and methanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and poly-vinyl alcohol (PVA) from SD Fine Chemicals. All other chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade. Triple distilled water filtered with 0.22 m membrane has been used in the experimental work.
2.2. Drug polymer interaction study
Drug polymer interaction study has been carried out by IR spectroscopy (Shimadzu, 8400-S) using KBr press palate technique. IR spectra of GPZ and GPZ loaded PLGA nanoparticles were studied .
2.3. Formulation optimization and preparation of nanoparticles
An attempt has been made to optimize the nanoparticle formulation using various formulation parameters like drug polymer ratios (GPZ:PLGA, 1:4, 1:2, 1:1, and 2:1) different organic solvents (methanol/dichloromethane), varying stirring speed (300-3000 rpm) and different surfactants (PVA/polysorbate-80) in a fixed concentration (0.5%, w/v) to prepare nanoparticles by modified emulsification solvent evaporation technique. PLGA and GPZ were dissolved in 25 ml of solvent using a vortex shaker to form a homogeneous solution of drug and polymer. This homogeneous solution was added slowly to 120 ml of 0.5% (w/v) aqueous surfactant solution using high pressure homogenizer (IKA, Japan) to prepare the emulsion. The emulsion formed was stirred on laboratory magnetic stirrer (REMI, India) for 6 h at 25 degC followed by centrifugation (REMI, India) for 20 min at 16,000 x g. After centrifugation the supernatant was discharged and the pallets obtained were washed by using the same volume of distilled water as of the supernatant and again centrifuged at 16,000 x g for 6 min. The washing process was repeated three times and the washed nanoparticles were subjected to freeze drying (HETO, Japan).
2.4. Entrapment efficiency and drug content
10 g of freeze died nanoparticles were weighed and dissolved in 10 ml of methanol and after suitable dilution analyzed by the UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Pharmaspec-1700). GPZ contents were determined from standard curve. (Subsequent study reveal that no absorbance interference from PLGA polymer under the same conditions.) GPZ entrapment efficiency (%) and GPZ contents in nanoparticles (% w/w) were calculated by Eqs. , , respectively :
2.5. Characterization of nanoparticles
2.5.1. Particle size determination
The mean particle size of the formulations was determined by particle size analyzer (Malvern S4700 PCS System, Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK). The analysis was performed at a scattering angle of 90deg and at a temperature of 25 degC using samples appropriately diluted with filtered water (0.22 m filter). For each sample, the mean diameter +- standard deviations of six determinations were calculated applying multimodal analysis. Values reported are the mean diameter +- standard deviation for two replicate samples.
2.5.2. Surface charge
Nanoparticles were characterized with respect to zeta (z) potential by using Laser Doppler Anemometry (Malvern Zetasizer IV, Malvern Instrments Ltd., Malvern, UK). All analyses were performed on samples appropriately diluted with 1 mM HEPES buffer (adjusted to pH 7.4 with 1 M HCl) in order to maintain a constant ionic strength. For each sample the mean value +- standard deviations of four determinations was established. Values reported are the mean value +- standard deviation for two replicate samples.
2.5.3. Scanning electron microscopy
Morphological evaluation of the nanoparticles was performed using scanning electron microscope (S-3000N, magnification = 5.0kx, WD = 33.3 mm). The nanoparticles were mounted on metal stubs and then coated with a layer of gold.
2.5.4. In vitro drug release studies
The in vitro drug release studies were performed with some modification in the method . Briefly, GPZ-loaded PLGA NPs (equivalent to 10 mg of GPZ) were suspended in 100 ml of phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, USP-XIV) in a glass bottle. The glass bottle was placed in a stirring bath (50 rpm), with temperature adjusted to 37 degC. At selected time intervals, 5.0 ml of the sample was removed and centrifuged at 20,000 rpm for 30 min. Aliquot was analyzed by UV-spectrophotometry at lmax of 271.5 nm. Pallets were resuspended in 5 ml of fresh phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) and added to the glass bottle.
2.5.5. Stability study
The selected formulations were subjected to accelerated stability studies to evaluate effect of stress conditions according to ICH guidelines. Formulations were packed in 0.044 mm dilaminated aluminum foil and subjected to elevated temperature and humidity conditions of 40deg +- 2 degC/75 +- 5% RH, 30deg +- 2 degC/65 +- 5% RH and also 25deg +- 2 degC/60 +- 5% RH. Samples were withdrawn at the end of 1, 3 and 6 months and evaluated for physical properties, drug content and in vitro drug release .
